ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP used for baked potato with toppings; Turkish-style variant with butter, cheese, and mixed toppings is not separately coded. Closest defensible match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Turkish-style baked potato dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 400 g, representing one large whole baked potato loaded with butter, cheese, corn, peas, olives, and condiments as typically served at Turkish kebab and shawarma stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Potato, baked, flesh and skin, without salt (FDC ID 170032); component toppings modelled from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for butter, cheddar cheese, sweetcorn, green peas, olives, and mayonnaise
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain baked potato (flesh and skin, without salt) nutritional values sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170032. Toppings (butter, cheddar cheese, sweetcorn, green peas, olives, mayonnaise, ketchup) modelled using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries. Final composite per-100g values represent a typical Turkish-style loaded baked potato at approximately 60% potato base and 40% mixed toppings by weight. Input record values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA data; values are broadly consistent and have been refined to USDA-anchored figures. Sodium at 200 mg/100g is plausible given ketchup, pickles, and cheese toppings. No Singapore HPB or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific Turkish-style dish; USDA FoodData Central is the highest-priority approved source available for this food.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Am J Clin Nutr. 2008;87(1):223S-236S. — Baked potato (Russet Burbank), mean GI 85; corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database entry for baked potato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 85 is the published mean GI for baked potato (Russet Burbank) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Am J Clin Nutr 2008;87:223S–236S, which draws on the University of Sydney GI Database. The Turkish-style loaded baked potato uses the same potato base; toppings (butter, cheese, peas, corn) modestly lower the effective GI but the potato dominates the glycaemic response. GI 85 is applied as a conservative upper-bound estimate consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (85 × 55.2 g) / 100 = 46.9. Available carbohydrate per serving = 13.8 g/100g × 400 g = 55.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 200 mg/100g (800 mg per 400 g serving) is at the upper threshold; condiment toppings (ketchup, pickles) add further hidden sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request condiments on the side, avoid pickles and ketchup, and note that a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium — one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit — partially offset by an estimated 1,520 mg potassium per serving which supports DASH-aligned blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 85) baked potato base with a glycemic load of approximately 46.9 per 400 g serving poses a significant postprandial glucose challenge
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, consume the potato skin to maximise fibre intake, pair with high-protein toppings such as grilled chicken to slow gastric emptying, and avoid ketchup and chilli sauce toppings which contribute additional refined sugars that further elevate postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.8 g/100g (15.2 g per 400 g serving) from butter, cheese, and mayonnaise toppings contributes meaningfully to daily saturated fat intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should substitute butter with extra-virgin olive oil, request reduced mayonnaise or replace with plain yogurt, and avoid sausage toppings which would substantially increase both saturated fat and sodium; the potato base itself provides potassium and vitamin C which are cardioprotective, but the topping profile requires modification for heart-healthy consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine, which may trigger inflammatory responses in RA-sensitive individuals; saturated fat from butter and cheese further contributes to pro-inflammatory load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor individual tolerance to nightshade vegetables, substitute butter with extra-virgin olive oil to shift the fat profile toward anti-inflammatory MUFAs, retain olive toppings for their anti-inflammatory benefit, and avoid sausage toppings which are processed red meat rich in arachidonic acid.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Turkish-Style Baked Potato (known in Turkey as Kumpir) is a whole large potato baked until the skin is crisp and the interior is fluffy, then split open and loaded with a variety of toppings. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at Turkish kebab and shawarma stalls and food courts. The standard toppings include butter mashed into the hot potato flesh, shredded cheddar cheese, sweetcorn, green peas, olives, pickles, ketchup, chilli sauce, and mayonnaise, creating a rich, savoury, and mildly tangy flavour profile with a creamy, soft interior contrasting with the crisp skin. A full serving weighs approximately 400 g. Nutritionally, the dish provides 137 kcal per 100 g, with 13.8 g carbohydrate, 7.5 g fat (3.8 g saturated), 3.7 g protein, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 200 mg sodium per 100 g. The glycemic index is high (GI 85) due to the baked potato base, making portion control critical for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is halal and gluten-free in its standard form. Clinically, the high GI and glycemic load (approximately 46.9 per 400 g serving) make it unsuitable for uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes without modification. Sodium content is borderline for hypertension management, though potassium from potato, peas, and corn partially offsets this. Saturated fat from butter, cheese, and mayonnaise is a concern for cardiovascular disease patients. Potato is a nightshade vegetable and may be problematic for rheumatoid arthritis patients sensitive to solanine.
common_user_queries: Is baked potato good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central plain baked potato data (FDC ID 170032) with topping components modelled from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. The Turkish-style loaded variant is not directly catalogued in any approved primary source. Input record values (GrabFood, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with USDA-derived estimates. Confidence is medium because the exact topping proportions vary by vendor and no Singapore HPB or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are USDA FoodData Central-adapted composite estimates for a Turkish-style loaded baked potato (approximately 60% potato base, 40% mixed toppings by weight), sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170032 (Potato, baked, flesh and skin, without salt) and USDA SR Legacy component entries for butter, cheddar cheese, sweetcorn, green peas, olives, and mayonnaise. The input GrabFood values were ai_generated at confidence 0.7 and have been refined against USDA FoodData Central data. USDA FoodData Central is the highest-priority approved source available for this food as no Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry exists for this Turkish-style dish. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Turkish-style composite dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database; individual component values exist but composite modelling for all micronutrients was not performed to avoid compounding estimation error. 3. Potassium estimated at 380 mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central values for baked potato flesh and skin (~535 mg/100g) blended with topping components; this estimate supports the hypertension DASH-alignment assessment. 4. GI of 85 applied from University of Sydney GI Database as cited in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Am J Clin Nutr 2008;87:223S–236S for baked Russet potato; toppings modestly lower effective GI but potato dominates glycaemic response. 5. The dish is described as Turkish-style as sold in Singapore kebab and shawarma restaurants; it is a localised adaptation of the Turkish street food Kumpir. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is sold in Singapore. 7. No SNOMED CT code applied — ontology honesty preserved. 8. Aliases consistency check: all string values in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Turkish-Style Baked Potato', a precise canonical English name. Multilingual names provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi satisfying the Singaporean dish requirement. Romanized local name 'Kumpir' included.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03301710, baked potato, exact match). FoodEx2 code A0CKP applied as closest_parent match with gap note. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. No codes invented.
